response.description_long:
Forkfleet runs a two-sided marketplace for used commercial kitchen equipment — ovens, ranges, refrigeration, prep tables, espresso machines — aimed at a market that historically cleared through auctioneers and local dealers at steep discounts. The structural problem it names is symmetric: closing or renovating restaurants dump equipment for little, while buyers face a used market with no condition standards and no delivery.

The product's answer is to standardize trust and logistics rather than just host listings. Sellers photograph equipment in the Forkfleet app and get a price estimate from comparable sales; a Forkfleet inspector then physically verifies condition — burners, compressors, door seals, temperature calibration — before the listing goes live with a graded condition report. Buyers see that report alongside financing options and a delivery quote, and Forkfleet coordinates freight through partner carriers, down to liftgate delivery and haul-away of the old equipment.

The economics are stated plainly: sellers pay an 8 percent commission per sale, buyers pay a flat service fee, and financing runs through a lending partner. Notably, nothing is consigned to a warehouse — equipment ships directly from seller to buyer — which keeps Forkfleet asset-light while the inspection layer still lets it stand behind a 30-day working guarantee on every purchase.

Traction markers are concrete for a marketplace of this vintage: launched in Chicago in 2019, expanded to 12 metro markets, over 5,000 items listed at any time, and a team of 45 headquartered in Chicago, IL.

The wedge is the inspection-plus-freight bundle. Listings alone would leave Forkfleet competing with classifieds and auction houses on price; graded condition reports and door-to-door delivery are the parts a restaurant operator cannot easily assemble alone, and they are what turns idle equipment from a liquidation problem into sellable inventory. The open question the site leaves is what standing in twelve markets costs in inspector coverage — but the model, as described, is a considered answer to a genuinely fragmented category.
